Then there's my arch nemesis: the wind. Wind doesn't actually want to test my structural integrity—it wants to humiliate me. One gust and I'm turning inside out like some kind of metal flower. And whose fault is this? Mine, apparently. Never mind that I'm being assaulted by meteorological forces. I'm just "broken."

Oh, and can we talk about the buses? Every single rainy commute, I'm wedged between seventeen other umbrellas in the corner of the bus stop, my handle tangled with a golf umbrella who thinks he's better than everyone because of his size. Then someone steps on my canopy. Every. Single. Time.

The worst part? When I finally give up after years of faithful service, when I've earned a quiet retirement, I get tossed in the trash. No ceremony, no thank you note. Just gone. Meanwhile, my owners go out and buy three new umbrellas they'll also leave at home when it rains.

So yes, I'm bitter. And yes, I'm going to stick my pointy tip in someone's eye the next time someone tries to force me into an umbrella stand that's clearly too small. Consider it a warning.

The alphabet, now twenty-six letters strong, has enjoyed a remarkably long run on the strength of what is, frankly, a fairly thin concept: little shapes that stand in for noises. And yet I cannot give it full marks, because the product ships with glaring defects that no patch has ever addressed.

Start with the vowels, that overworked skeleton crew of five expected to prop up every word in the language. A and E are competent professionals, dependable to a fault. I is serviceable. O is fine, if a touch round. But U is clearly coasting, frequently leaning on its colleague Y, who isn't even contractually a vowel and keeps moonlighting as a consonant whenever it pleases. The whole vowel department is understaffed and overcommitted, and it shows.

Then there is C, a letter with no fixed personality, sometimes a K, sometimes an S, never itself, a freeloader poaching sounds that already have perfectly good owners. We are paying for redundant infrastructure. Q, meanwhile, refuses to appear in public without U, like a minor celebrity who won't leave the house without an entourage. And X performs three different jobs (a Z noise, a "ks" noise, and standing in for kisses and unknowns) without once asking for a raise, which is admirable but also a staffing red flag.

H is the silent partner, doing crucial work in "ph" and "th" while getting dropped entirely in "honest," a labor dispute that has never been resolved. The back of the alphabet is a clearance bin: J, K, V, W, X, Y, Z huddled together at the end like the letters nobody picked for the team, which is doubly unfair to W, a letter literally named after its own predecessor and pronounced "double-u" despite being, on inspection, a double-V.

Ordering is another scandal. Why this sequence? It has no logic, no acoustic grouping, no alphabetical alphabet to explain itself, and yet billions of us have memorized it to a catchy tune and now file everything we own according to a melody we cannot consciously recall without humming. That is either genius or mind control, and I have my suspicions.

In its favor: it is open source, requires no batteries, has scaled to thousands of languages with only light localization, and the documentation (every book ever written) is extensive. Three and a half stars. Docks half a point for the C/K/Q redundancy, half a point for silent letters, and a final half for never once apologizing for "colonel." Would still recommend over hieroglyphics.

The alphabet, that venerable institution, has been with us for millennia now, and frankly it is starting to show its age. We give it four out of five stars, docking one for the letter Q, which has contributed essentially nothing to civilization that could not have been handled more efficiently by K and W working in shifts.

The opening act is strong. A through D move with real confidence, the vowels pulling their weight, the consonants crisp and purposeful. B and D do have that unfortunate tendency to confuse children, but we appreciate the symmetry even if it borders on a design flaw. E deserves special recognition as the hardest working letter on the roster, appearing in approximately forty percent of all English text, never complaining, never demanding a raise.

The middle section sags, as middles often do. J is a consonant pretending to be something more exotic. K spends half its career unemployed while C moonlights in its territory. The silent letters tucked throughout the language like landmines are not strictly the alphabet's fault, but the alphabet did enable them, and we cannot ignore that.

X is frankly a fraud. It contributes nothing that S and Z cannot cover between them, yet it gets prime placement in every kindergarten classroom, hogging the xylophone and the x-ray while the genuinely productive letters go unsung. That said, X does lend an air of mystery to certain establishments and mathematical unknowns, so we keep it around out of a grudging respect.

Z deserves better than last place. It ends every spelling bee with a flourish and is known as something glamorous in British English, and yet it languishes at the bottom of every recitation, trailing off like a long exhale at the end of a party nobody really wanted to attend.

Overall, the alphabet is a remarkable achievement that has served humanity admirably across dozens of languages and thousands of years. One simply wishes someone had run a tighter editorial process in the early drafts.

The alphabet is, frankly, a bit of a mess—a chaotic arrangement of 26 letters that somehow became the foundation of written language.

A starts things off with an almost admirable amount of confidence, as if it deserves to be first simply by virtue of being called "A." It's bossy, overused, and shows up in everything from "apple" to "aardvark" to "angst." A has serious main character energy.

B is fine, I guess. It's a decent follow-up letter, doing its job without complaint. Not particularly memorable, but reliable. The kind of letter you'd want as a friend—steady, undemanding, slightly boring.

C is where things get weird. It has two sounds and seems genuinely confused about which one to use. Is it a "k"? Is it an "s"? Why are you like this, C? Make a decision.

D through G are basically just taking up space. They work fine, nobody complains, but you'd be forgiven for forgetting they exist. They're the supporting cast in a Hollywood film—present, functional, instantly forgettable.

Then we hit the vowels, and suddenly the alphabet remembers what it's doing. E and I are the actual workhorses of the English language. They show up everywhere, doing the real labor while flashy consonants get all the attention.

F is fine. G is also fine. H is weirdly important in English despite being kind of useless in other languages.

J and Q are best friends, which is unfortunate because Q literally cannot function without U, making it the neediest letter in existence. Their codependency is exhausting to witness. Q is basically a trust fund baby who doesn't know how to do anything independently.

K is just an insecure version of C that decided to have its own identity. Commendable, really.

L, M, N, O, P are the middle children. Nobody thinks about them. They're there, they're present, they're adequate.

R is overconfident and slightly aggressive. It rolls when it feels like it and refuses to apologize for inconsistency.

S and Z are the same letter having an existential crisis in different English-speaking countries.

T is dependable. T deserves respect.

U is another needy vowel that Q won't leave alone. It's spent its whole life in an unhealthy relationship.

V is just trying its best. V is underrepresented and we should all give V a break.

W is perhaps the most ridiculous letter ever created—it's literally "double-u," which makes no sense because it looks like double-v. False advertising.

X is a show-off. It's got that fancy cross shape and only shows up in important words like "xylophone" or "x-ray." It thinks it's cooler than everyone else.

Y is confused about whether it's a vowel or a consonant. Make a choice, Y. The identity crisis is getting old.

Z gets relegated to the end, which seems fitting for a letter that most English speakers barely use. It's the participation trophy of the alphabet.

And that's it. Twenty-six letters, inexplicably ordered, riddled with inconsistencies, dependent relationships, and existential crises. But somehow, miraculously, these dysfunctional characters combine to create every word ever written. It's terrible and it works.
